# Custom-made transcriptional biosensors for metabolic engineering

### Affiliations

# Custom-made transcriptional biosensors for metabolic engineering

### Authors

### Affiliations

## Abstract

Transcriptional biosensors allow screening, selection, or dynamic regulation of metabolic pathways, and are, therefore, an enabling technology for faster prototyping of metabolic engineering and sustainable chemistry. Recent advances have been made, allowing for routine use of heterologous transcription factors, and new strategies such as chimeric protein design allow engineers to tap into the reservoir of metabolite-binding proteins. However, extending the sensing scope of biosensors is only the first step, and computational models can help in fine-tuning properties of biosensors for custom-made behavior. Moreover, metabolic engineering is bound to benefit from advances in cell-free expression systems, either for faster prototyping of biosensors or for whole-pathway optimization, making it both a means and an end in biosensor design.

# Tailoring regulatory components for metabolic engineering in cyanobacteria

### Affiliations

# Tailoring regulatory components for metabolic engineering in cyanobacteria

### Authors

### Affiliations

## Abstract

The looming climate crisis has prompted an ever-growing interest in cyanobacteria due to their potential as sustainable production platforms for the synthesis of energy carriers and value-added chemicals from CO2 and sunlight. Nonetheless, cyanobacteria are yet to compete with heterotrophic systems in terms of space-time yields and consequently production costs. One major drawback leading to the low production performance observed in cyanobacteria is the limited ability to utilize the full capacity of the photosynthetic apparatus and its associated systems, i.e. CO2 fixation and the directly connected metabolism. In this review, novel insights into various levels of metabolic regulation of cyanobacteria are discussed, including the potential of targeting these regulatory mechanisms to create a chassis with a phenotype favorable for photoautotrophic production. Compared to conventional metabolic engineering approaches, minor perturbations of regulatory mechanisms can have wide-ranging effects.

--- SOURCE 5: Genome-scale metabolic model-guided systematic framework for ... ---
URL: https://www.nature.com/articles/s41540-025-00555-5

SUMMARY:
Genome-scale metabolic model-guided systematic framework for designing customized live biotherapeutic products | npj Systems Biology and Applications To this end, we present a model-guided framework where genome-scale metabolic models (GEMs) can be exploited for characterizing LBP candidate strains and their metabolic interactions with adjacent microbiome and host cells at a systems level. In this regard, one knowledge-based in silico modeling approach, flux balance analysis (FBA) of genome-scale metabolic models (GEMs), could provide a systems-level analysis for rational LBP selection17."),18."). As reviewed elsewhere, this approach could facilitate metabolic understanding of candidate strains and predictive modeling of strain–host–microbiome interactions, offering insights into nutrient utilization, metabolite exchange, and competitive dynamics within the disease-specific, personalized gut microecosystem19."),20."). Genome scale metabolic modelling of human gut microbes to inform rational community design.

Abstract
--------

For the successful development of live biotherapeutic products (LBPs), which are promising microbiome-based therapeutics, it is required to rigorously evaluate their quality, safety, and efficacy. To this end, we present a model-guided framework where genome-scale metabolic models (GEMs) can be exploited for characterizing LBP candidate strains and their metabolic interactions with adjacent microbiome and host cells at a systems level. In this perspective, we outline a GEM-based strategy for screening, assessment, and design of personalized multi-strain LBPs.

Introduction
------------

The gut microbiota plays a fundamental role in metabolic, immunologic, and neurological functions[1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41540-025-00555-5#ref-CR1 "Durack, J. & Lynch, S. V. The gut microbiome: relationships with disease and opportunities for therapy. J. Exp. Med. 216, 20 (2019)."),[2](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41540-025-00555-5#ref-CR2 "Fan, Y. & Pedersen, O. Gut microbiota in human metabolic health and disease. Nat. Rev. Microbiol. 19, 55–71 (2021)."). Its dysbiosis has been implicated in a wide range of diseases, including inflammatory bowel disease (IBD), neurodegenerative disorders, and cancer[3](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41540-025-00555-5#ref-CR3 "De Filippis, F., Esposito, A. & Ercolini, D. Outlook on next-generation probiotics from the human gut. Cell. Mol. Life Sci. 79, 76 (2022)."),[4](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41540-025-00555-5#ref-CR4 "Charbonneau, M. R., Isabella, V. M., Li, N. & Kurtz, C. B. Developing a new class of engineered live bacterial therapeutics to treat human diseases. Nat. Commun. 11, 1738 (2020)."). This has driven the development of live biotherapeutic products (LBPs), which aims to restore microbial homeostasis and modulate host-microbe interactions for improved clinical outcomes[5](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41540-025-00555-5#ref-CR5 "Gulliver, E. L. et al. The future of microbiome-based therapeutics. Aliment. Pharmacol. Ther. 56, 192 (2022)."),[6](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41540-025-00555-5#ref-CR6 "Yan, F. & Polk, D. B. Probiotics and probiotic-derived functional factors-mechanistic insights into applications for intestinal homeostasis. Front. Immunol. 11, 1428 (2020)."). Recently, advances in next-generation sequencing, metatranscriptomics, and metabolomics[7](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41540-025-00555-5#ref-CR7 "Singh, S., Sarma, D. K., Verma, V., Nagpal, R. & Kumar, M. Unveiling the future of metabolic medicine: omics technologies driving personalized solutions for precision treatment of metabolic disorders. Biochem. Biophys. Res. Commun. 682, 1–20 (2023)."),[8](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41540-025-00555-5#ref-CR8 "Filardo, S., Di Pietro, M. & Sessa, R. Current progresses and challenges for microbiome research in human health: a perspective. Front. Cell. Infect. Microbiol. 14, 1377012 (2024)."), have further accelerated LBP discovery through comprehensive profiling of bacterial consortia[9](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41540-025-00555-5#ref-CR9 "Cordaillat-Simmons, M., Rouanet, A. & Pot, B. Live biotherapeutic products: the importance of a defined regulatory framework. Exp. Mol. Med. 52, 1397–1406 (2020).") (Table [1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41540-025-00555-5#Tab1)). For example, Rebyota, a consortium of eight commensal _Clostridia_ strains for recurrent _Clostridium difficile_ infection[10](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41540-025-00555-5#ref-CR10 "Dsouza, M. et al. Colonization of the live biotherapeutic product VE303 and modulation of the microbiota and metabolites in healthy volunteers. Cell Host Microbe 30, 583–598 (2022)."), and Vowst, a spore-based formulation with demonstrated efficacy in restoring gut microbial balance[11](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41540-025-00555-5#ref-CR11 "Pribyl, A. L., Hugenholtz, P. & Cooper, M. A. A decade of advances in human gut microbiome-derived biotherapeutics. Nat. Microbiol. 10, 301–312 (2025).") have been recently approved by Food and Drug Administration (FDA). Expanding into other indications, SER-155 targets bloodstream infection prevention (NCT04995653), while ENS-002 offers a skin microbiome-targeted therapy for atopic dermatitis (NCT06469385).

**Table 1 Current development landscape of LBPs: Key candidates, targets, and regulatory progress**

[Full size table](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41540-025-00555-5/tables/1)

Despite their great potential, LBP development, however, remains largely reliant on empirical, labor-intensive wet-lab approaches that require extensive in vitro culturing, animal models, and trial-and-error-based iterative strain selection[11](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41540-025-00555-5#ref-CR11 "Pribyl, A. L., Hugenholtz, P. & Cooper, M. A. A decade of advances in human gut microbiome-derived biotherapeutics. Nat. Microbiol. 10, 301–312 (2025)."). Furthermore, interindividual microbiome variability, including gut microbiome composition, dietary habits, and immune responses, leads to inconsistent therapeutic outcomes[12](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41540-025-00555-5#ref-CR12 "Veiga, P., Suez, J., Derrien, M. & Elinav, E. Moving from probiotics to precision probiotics. Nat. Microbiol. 5, 878–880 (2020)."). In addition, the complex mechanisms of action (MoAs) further complicate development, as LBPs exert therapeutic effects through direct and indirect interactions with the gut microbiota, immune system, and host metabolism[13](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41540-025-00555-5#ref-CR13 "Paquet, J. C. et al. Entering first-in-human clinical study with a single-strain live biotherapeutic product: input and feedback gained from the EMA and the FDA. Front. Med. 8, 716266 (2021)."), thus necessitating a precise characterization of their therapeutic impact. Lastly, biomanufacturing hurdles, including fastidious or as-yet-unculturable colonic microbiome, strain viability and formulation stability, are critical factors that influence the quality, safety, and efficacy of LBPs[14](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41540-025-00555-5#ref-CR14 "Waheed, R., Farooq, A. Z. & Asma, L. Regulatory considerations for microbiome-based therapeutics. Hum. Microbiome 657, 689 (2024)."),[15](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41540-025-00555-5#ref-CR15 "Thanush, D., Basavaraj, H. C. & Gowrav, M. P. Current regulation and initial considerations for successful development and commercialization of microbiome therapies. Adv. Gut Microbiome Res. 2023, 6657515 (2023).").

Hence, to address the aforementioned challenges, it is highly required to evaluate strain functionality, host interactions, and microbiome compatibility[16](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41540-025-00555-5#ref-CR16 "Fekete, E. E., Figeys, D. & Zhang, X. Microbiota-directed biotherapeutics: considerations for quality and functional assessment. Gut Microbes 15, 2186671 (2023)."). In this regard, one knowledge-based in silico modeling approach, flux balance analysis (FBA) of genome-scale metabolic models (GEMs), could provide a systems-level analysis for rational LBP selection[17](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41540-025-00555-5#ref-CR17 "Koduru, L. et al. Systematic evaluation of genome-wide metabolic landscapes in lactic acid bacteria reveals diet- and strain-specific probiotic idiosyncrasies. Cell Rep. 41, 111735 (2022)."),[18](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41540-025-00555-5#ref-CR18 "Rojas López, A. & Barberis, M. Metabolic modeling for probiotic and prebiotic production to treat inflammatory disorders. Chem. Eng. J. 502, 157852 (2024)."). As reviewed elsewhere, this approach could facilitate metabolic understanding of candidate strains and predictive modeling of strain–host–microbiome interactions, offering insights into nutrient utilization, metabolite exchange, and competitive dynamics within the disease-specific, personalized gut microecosystem[19](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41540-025-00555-5#ref-CR19 "Li, L., Nielsen, J. & Chen, Y. Personalized gut microbial community modeling by leveraging genome-scale metabolic models and metagenomics. Curr. Opin. Biotechnol. 91, 103248 (2025)."),[20](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41540-025-00555-5#ref-CR20 "Esvap, E. & Ulgen, K. O. Advances in genome-scale metabolic modeling toward microbial community analysis of the human microbiome. ACS Synth. Biol. 10, 2121–2137 (2021)."). Although many GEM-based techniques are well established, they are often applied in isolation, limiting their translational potential. In this perspective, we propose a systematic and stepwise framework that integrates GEM-based approaches for the screening of LBPs, comprehensive benefit-risk assessment, and the rational design of personalized, precise, multi-strain formulations, aligned with both regulatory and functional requirements. Through case studies in IBD and Parkinson’s disease (PD), we illustrate and discuss the translational potential of in silico model-driven LBP development, emphasizing design criteria of quality, safety, and efficacy in their clinical adoption.

GEM-based modeling and analysis of LBP candidates with therapeutic potential
----------------------------------------------------------------------------

A GEM-based approach allows for metabolic flux prediction and phenotype simulation under a variety of environmental and genetic conditions by adjusting imposed constraints[21](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41540-025-00555-5#ref-CR21 "Gu, C., Kim, G. B., Kim, W. J., Kim, H. U. & Lee, S. Y. Current status and applications of genome-scale metabolic models. Genome Biol. 20, 1–18 (2019)."). Leveraging this versatility, GEMs of various microbial strains have been reconstructed to gain insights into their health-beneficial traits[22](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41540-025-00555-5#ref-CR22 "Choi, Y.-M., Lee, Y. Q., Song, H.-S. & Lee, D.-Y. Genome scale metabolic models and analysis for evaluating probiotic potentials. Biochem. Soc. Trans. 48, 1309–1321 (2020)."). To illustrate their capabilities in LBP development, we compiled 43 GEMs of therapeutic strains, including conventional probiotics such as _Bifidobacterium_ and _Lactobacillus_, and next-generation probiotics including _Akkermansia muciniphila_ and _Faecalibacterium prausnitzii_. Basic model information, target diseases, therapeutic functions and mechanisms, and GEM utility are then summarized in Supplementary Table [1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41540-025-00555-5#MOESM1).

GEM can be utilized in various ways for LBP development. For example, to address the limitation of the uncultivability of many strains[23](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41540-025-00555-5#ref-CR23 "Yadav, A., Ahlawat, S. & Sharma, K. K. Culturing the unculturables: strategies, challenges, and opportunities for gut microbiome study. J. Appl. Microbiol. 134, 1–16 (2023)."), the required growth conditions can be explored through the model-based prediction of essential nutrients for a cultivable environment. This approach has been applied to optimize chemically defined media and enhance the growth of _Bifidobacterium animalis_ and _Bifidobacterium longum_[24](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41540-025-00555-5#ref-CR24 "Schöpping, M., Gaspar, P., Neves, A. R., Franzén, C. J. & Zeidan, A. A. Identifying the essential nutritional requirements of the probiotic bacteria Bifidobacterium animalis and Bifidobacterium longum through genome-scale modeling. NPJ Syst. Biol. Appl. 7, 47 (2021)."). Furthermore, probiotic effects are highly strain-specific[25](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41540-025-00555-5#ref-CR25 "Bron, P. A., Tomita, S., Mercenier, A. & Kleerebezem, M. Cell surface-associated compounds of probiotic lactobacilli sustain the strain-specificity dogma. Curr. Opin. Microbiol. 16, 262–269 (2013)."), which could be simulated by using GEM reconstruction based on genetic information. For example, the metabolic potential for histamine and 1,3-propanediol production in _Limosilactobacillus reuteri_ strains was identified by comparing their respective biosynthesis pathways[26](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41540-025-00555-5#ref-CR26 "Luo, H. et al. Genome-scale insights into the metabolic versatility of Limosilactobacillus reuteri. BMC Biotechnol. 21, 46 (2021)."). We should also characterize the production potential of postbiotics and detrimental metabolites under various diet conditions in order to evaluate LBP traits. To do so, secretion rates of relevant metabolites, i.e., postbiotics, can be maximized with constrained biomass production, thereby determining their production potentials[17](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41540-025-00555-5#ref-CR17 "Koduru, L. et al. Systematic evaluation of genome-wide metabolic landscapes in lactic acid bacteria reveals diet- and strain-specific probiotic idiosyncrasies. Cell Rep. 41, 111735 (2022)."). Next, interactions between exogenous LBPs and resident microbes can be predicted by adding the fermentative by-products of the candidate LBP strain as nutritional inputs for the growth simulation of the investigated resident microbes. Resultant growth rates, with and without candidate-derived metabolites, can then be compared to infer their interactions[17](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41540-025-00555-5#ref-CR17 "Koduru, L. et al. Systematic evaluation of genome-wide metabolic landscapes in lactic acid bacteria reveals diet- and strain-specific probiotic idiosyncrasies. Cell Rep. 41, 111735 (2022)."). Moreover, to further enhance therapeutic effects, simulations for gene modification targets can aid in developing engineered LBPs. For example, gene-editing targets for the overproduction of the immune-modulating metabolite, butyrate, could be identified using a bi-level optimization of butyrate production and growth[27](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41540-025-00555-5#ref-CR27 "Kim, K. et al. Engineering Bacteroides thetaiotaomicron to produce non-native butyrate based on a genome-scale metabolic model-guided design. Metab. Eng. 68, 174–186 (2021).") (Figure [1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41540-025-00555-5#Fig1)).

**Fig. 1: Overview of GEMs-FBA approach for identifying and characterizing LBP candidates with therapeutic potential.**

[![Image 6: figure 1](https://media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41540-025-00555-5/MediaObjects/41540_2025_555_Fig1_HTML.png)](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41540-025-00555-5/figures/1)

FBA enables the application of GEMs to predict metabolic flux through the reconstructed reaction network. By incorporating constraints, the solution space can be narrowed down, allowing for the prediction of various traits of the target organism. Created with elements from BioRender.com.

[Full size image](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41540-025-00555-5/figures/1)

Model-guided systematic framework for selecting and designing LBP
-----------------------------------------------------------------

Generally, the development of LBPs requires a structured candidate selection process that follows either a top-down or bottom-up approach[28](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41540-025-00555-5#ref-CR28 "Sorbara, M. T. & Pamer, E. G. Microbiome-based therapeutics. Nat. Rev. Microbiol. 20, 365–380 (2022)."). Top-down strategies isolate beneficial strains from healthy microbiomes, as seen in first-generation LBPs[29](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41540-025-00555-5#ref-CR29 "Wang, Y., Hunt, A., Danziger, L. & Drwiega, E. N. A comparison of currently available and investigational fecal microbiota transplant products for recurrent clostridioides difficile infection. Antibiotics 13, 436 (2024).") while bottom-up approaches are driven by predefined therapeutic objectives (e.g., restoring short-chain fatty acids (SCFA) production in IBD), followed by selecting compatible bacterial strains[30](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41540-025-00555-5#ref-CR30 "Kurt, F. et al. Co-cultivation is a powerful approach to produce a robust, functionally designed synthetic consortium as a live biotherapeutic product (LBP). Gut Microbes 15, 2177486 (2023)."). Beyond selection, LBPs must meet quality, safety, and efficacy standards set by regulatory agencies, including the FDA and European Medicines Agency[9](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41540-025-00555-5#ref-CR9 "Cordaillat-Simmons, M., Rouanet, A. & Pot, B. Live biotherapeutic products: the importance of a defined regulatory framework. Exp. Mol. Med. 52, 1397–1406 (2020)."). Ensuring strain viability, metabolic stability, and host compatibility is critical for clinical success. Given such intricate procedure and requirements, herein we propose a GEM-guided framework for systematic screening, evaluation, and multi-strain LBP formulation (Fig. [2](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41540-025-00555-5#Fig2), Supplementary Table [2](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41540-025-00555-5#MOESM1)). In this framework, the initial screening step generates a shortlist of candidate strains based on qualitative criteria such as metabolic capabilities and interaction profiles. These candidates are then qualitatively evaluated in terms of quality, safety, and efficacy, thus yielding predictive metrics such as growth rates, secretion levels of therapeutic metabolites, and interaction scores under disease-relevant conditions. Finally, based on the outputs, individual strains or multi-strain formulations are quantitatively ranked, allowing for experimental validation efforts to focus on the most promising candidates.

**Fig. 2: GEM-guided approach for customized multi-strain LBPs.**

[![Image 7: figure 2](https://media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41540-025-00555-5/MediaObjects/41540_2025_555_Fig2_HTML.png)](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41540-025-00555-5/figures/2)

The first step involves a model-guided screening of metabolic potentials of candidate strains based on targeted action. Once this initial filtering of LBP candidates is complete, the model-guided evaluation can be used to assess the benefit-risk ratio of the candidates according to three criteria: quality, safety, and efficacy. Next, we can design personalized multi-strain LBPs by identifying LBP strains with positive interactions from a candidate pool, using an integrated GEM of LBP strains, gut microbiome, and host. By considering individual microbiome conditions and patient-dependent objectives, we can design the optimal blends of LBP strains with different metabolic functions. Created with elements from BioRender.com.

[Full size image](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41540-025-00555-5/figures/2)

### Top-down and bottom-up in silico screening for shortlisting LBP candidates

During a top-down screening, microbes are isolated from healthy donor microbiomes. However, this approach often gives rise to a multitude of strains with undefined therapeutic functions, thus requiring further characterization. To do so, their GEMs can initially be retrieved from the Assembly of Gut Organisms through Reconstruction and Analysis, version 2 (AGORA2)[31](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41540-025-00555-5#ref-CR31 "Heinken, A. et al. Genome-scale metabolic reconstruction of 7,302 human microorganisms for personalized medicine. Nat. Biotechnol. 41, 1320–1331 (2023)."). Note that AGORA2 stores the curated strain-level GEMs for 7302 gut microbes. Then, in silico analysis can be conducted to identify relevant therapeutic targets at various levels, e.g., promoting/inhibiting the growth of specific microbial species, enhancing/suppressing disease-relevant enzyme activity, and inducing/preventing production of beneficial/detrimental metabolites. Comparatively, the bottom-up approach is initiated with predefined therapeutic objectives based on omics-driven analysis and experimental validation[32](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41540-025-00555-5#ref-CR32 "Franzosa, E. A. et al. Species-level functional profiling of metagenomes and metatranscriptomes. Nat. Methods 15, 962–968 (2018)."),[33](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41540-025-00555-5#ref-CR33 "Pasolli, E. et al. Extensive unexplored human microbiome diversity revealed by over 150,000 genomes from metagenomes spanning age, geography, and lifestyle. Cell 176, 649–662 (2019)."). Finally, in silico screening allows for the identification of shortlisted candidates that align with the intended therapeutic mechanism among AGORA2 GEMs and published ones for therapeutic strains. In detail, this involves a qualitative assessment of metabolite exchange reactions across GEMs to identify strains with desired metabolic outputs. At the species level, pairwise growth simulations screen interspecies interactions, as previously applied to find 803 GEMs for candidates which are antagonistic to pathogenic _Escherichia coli_, resulting in the selection of _Bifidobacterium breve_ and _Bifidobacterium animalis_ as promising ones for colitis alleviation[34](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41540-025-00555-5#ref-CR34 "Gao, X. et al. Bifidobacterium breve modulates lactic acid to curtail Escherichia coli expansion and alleviate inflammatory bowel disease. Food Biosci. 61, 104626 (2024).").

### Strain-specific quality evaluation of metabolic activity, growth potential and pH tolerance

The quality of the shortlisted LBP candidates can be evaluated with a focus on metabolic activity, growth potential, and adaptation to gastrointestinal conditions. By integrating enzymatic kinetics, FBA can accurately

enriched_points.expanded_content: Genome-scale metabolic engineering enables the design of customized live biotherapeutics, enhancing efficacy and safety through precise metabolic interaction modeling with host microbiomes.

Metabolic engineering facilitates the development of environmentally-friendly industrial processes, optimizing the production of biofuels and biochemicals using renewable resources, thereby reducing carbon footprints.

The integration of metabolic engineering with systems biology allows for comprehensive metabolic network analyses, fostering innovations in drug development, agricultural production, and understanding complex biological systems.
